title: Caching at Every Layer
description: Follow one request from the browser outward through every cache it can hit — the HTTP cache (`Cache-Control`, `ETag`, `Last-Modified`, revalidation), the CDN edge, the client query cache (React Query / SWR), and server-side memoization — then face the hard part: invalidation. Covers strong vs. weak validators, `stale-while-revalidate`, cache stampedes and request coalescing, negative caching, and cache keys. Every explanation proves its answer against the relevant RFC.

questions.title: Who coined the "two hard things in Computer Science" quip?
questions.description: Complete the attribution: *"There are only two hard things in Computer Science: cache invalidation and naming things."*

options.text: Phil Karlton
Donald Knuth
Leslie Lamport
Martin Fowler

questions.explanation: The line is attributed to **Phil Karlton**, a Netscape engineer. It survives because both halves are real: a name is a cache of intent that goes stale when meaning drifts, and invalidation is *deciding when a stored copy no longer reflects the truth* — the theme of this entire quiz. Knuth gave us "premature optimization is the root of all evil"; Lamport's fame is distributed systems ("a distributed system is one where a computer you didn't know existed can render your own unusable"); Fowler popularized the quote but did not originate it.

questions.title: What does `Cache-Control: no-cache` actually permit?
questions.description: A response carries `Cache-Control: no-cache`.

options.text: The cache may store the response, but must revalidate with the origin before reusing it
The cache must not store the response anywhere
The response may be reused freely for the rest of the session
Only the browser cache is forbidden; shared caches may reuse it

questions.explanation: The name is a famous trap. `no-cache` does **not** mean "don't store" — it means *store, but don't reuse without successful revalidation*. The cache keeps the copy and, on the next request, sends a conditional request (`If-None-Match` / `If-Modified-Since`); if the origin answers `304 Not Modified`, the stored body is served without re-downloading. That's why `no-cache` paired with an `ETag` is efficient: you skip the body, not the round trip. The directive that forbids storage entirely is `no-store` — a different question.

questions.title: When does `no-store` belong on a response?

options.text: A bank statement page that must never be written to disk or memory by any cache
A logo that changes once a year and should be cached for a long time
An API response that should be reused but checked for freshness each time
A fingerprinted JS bundle that will never change under its URL

questions.explanation: `no-store` is the strongest directive: *no part of the request or response may be stored in any cache* — not the browser disk cache, not an intermediary. It's for genuinely sensitive, non-reusable content (statements, one-time tokens). The other options each want the opposite of "don't store": the logo wants a long `max-age`; the API wants `no-cache` (store + always revalidate); the fingerprinted bundle wants `max-age=31536000, immutable`. Reaching for `no-store` on cacheable content is a common, silent performance leak.

questions.title: What does `Cache-Control: private` tell caches?

options.text: Only a single-user (browser) cache may store it; shared caches must not
The response is encrypted and must be served over HTTPS
No cache of any kind may store the response
It may only be stored by shared caches, not the browser

questions.explanation: `private` is about *who* may store, not about encryption. It marks the response as intended for one user, so a **shared** cache (CDN, proxy) must not keep it, while the user's own browser cache may. `public` is the inverse hint: it lets a shared cache store a response that would otherwise be held back (for example, one tied to an `Authorization` request). Confusing `private` with "secret/encrypted" is the misconception baked into the distractors — `private` provides no confidentiality, only a storage-audience rule.

questions.title: Why is the CDN serving one user's data to everyone, and what's the fix?
questions.description:
An app puts each logged-in user's dashboard behind a CDN. Users start seeing *other people's* names. The origin sends:

```http
Cache-Control: max-age=60
```

options.text: The response is cacheable by the shared CDN with no `private`; per-user responses need `Cache-Control: private, no-store` (or `private`)
`max-age` is too high; lowering it to `10` fixes the leak
The CDN needs `Vary: Accept-Encoding` added
The browser cache is leaking; add `no-cache`

questions.explanation: A bare `max-age=60` with no `private` is a *shared-cacheable* response. The CDN stores the first user's dashboard under the URL key and replays it to the next visitor for 60 seconds — a classic cross-user data leak. The fix is to scope storage: `private` forbids the shared cache from keeping it, and `no-store` is safer still for sensitive pages. Lowering `max-age` only shrinks the leak window; `Vary: Accept-Encoding` controls compression variants, not user identity; blaming the browser cache misreads the layer — the browser cache is per-user and was never the problem.

questions.title: How long does each cache treat this response as fresh?
questions.description: A response is sent with `Cache-Control: max-age=60, s-maxage=600`.

options.text: Browser cache: 60s; shared cache (CDN/proxy): 600s
Both caches: 60s — `s-maxage` is only a hint
Both caches: 600s — the larger value always wins
Browser cache: 600s; shared cache: 60s

questions.explanation: `s-maxage` overrides `max-age` **for shared caches only** and is ignored by a private (browser) cache. So the browser honours `max-age=60` while the CDN honours `s-maxage=600`. This split is the bread-and-butter of edge caching: keep the browser copy short so users see updates quickly, but let the edge hold a longer copy to shield the origin. (`s-maxage` also implies `proxy-revalidate`.) The "larger value wins" and "only a hint" distractors miss that the two numbers target different cache tiers on purpose.

questions.title: Which numeric HTTP status lets the cache reuse its copy *without* re-sending the body?
questions.description: A cache sends `If-None-Match` with the stored `ETag`. The representation is unchanged.
questions.explanation: `304 Not Modified` is the whole point of validators. The origin confirms "your copy is still good" in a tiny headers-only response, and the cache serves the body it already has. This is why `no-cache` and `max-age=0, must-revalidate` are cheap when paired with an `ETag` or `Last-Modified`: you pay one round trip but save the payload. Contrast `200 OK` (full body re-sent) and `412 Precondition Failed` (used for write preconditions like `If-Match`, not GET revalidation).

questions.title: Which constraint does a correct `304 Not Modified` response obey?

options.text: It must not include a message body; the cache reuses the stored representation
It must repeat the full body so the cache can verify the bytes
It must drop the `ETag` header to signal the match
It must include `Content-Length: 0` and an empty body block

questions.explanation: A `304` is deliberately bodyless — re-sending the payload would defeat revalidation. The origin returns status + updated headers (it *should* keep `ETag`, and may refresh `Cache-Control`, `Date`, etc.), and the cache serves the body it already stored. Requiring a body, or stripping the `ETag`, would break the mechanism. Note this differs from `204 No Content`, which also has no body but means "request succeeded, nothing to return" rather than "your cached copy is still valid."

questions.title: When a cache revalidates a stored GET response, which header pairing does it use?

options.text: `If-None-Match` carries the `ETag`; `If-Modified-Since` carries the `Last-Modified` date
`If-Match` carries the `ETag`; `If-Unmodified-Since` carries the `Last-Modified` date
`If-None-Match` carries the `Last-Modified` date; `If-Modified-Since` carries the `ETag`
The client sends `ETag`; the server replies with `If-Modified-Since`

questions.explanation: Revalidation echoes back the validators the cache stored: the `ETag` returns in `If-None-Match`, and the `Last-Modified` date returns in `If-Modified-Since`. A match yields `304`. The `If-Match` / `If-Unmodified-Since` pair looks similar but serves the opposite purpose — *write* preconditions for optimistic concurrency ("only PUT if it hasn't changed"), where a mismatch yields `412`. And validators flow server→client first (`ETag`, `Last-Modified` are response headers); the client only sends the conditional `If-*` variants.

questions.title: What does the `W/` prefix on an ETag signify?
questions.description:
An origin sends:

```http
ETag: W/"7e3a91"
```

options.text: A weak validator: the representations are semantically equivalent but may differ byte-for-byte
The ETag is a wildcard that matches any request
The response was served over a WebSocket
The ETag is weakly encrypted and should be treated as untrusted

questions.explanation: `W/` marks a **weak** ETag: two representations sharing it are *semantically equivalent* but not guaranteed identical at the byte level. That's useful when trivial differences shouldn't bust the cache — e.g. the same page gzipped vs. not, or with a regenerated timestamp comment. A **strong** ETag (no prefix) promises byte-for-byte equality. The prefix is neither a wildcard (`*` is the wildcard in `If-None-Match: *`) nor anything about transport or encryption.

questions.title: Why must a range request's validator be a **strong** ETag, not a weak one?
questions.description: A client resumes a partial download with `Range` + `If-Range`.

options.text: Combining new bytes with already-held bytes requires byte-for-byte identity, which only a strong validator guarantees
Weak ETags expire faster than strong ones
Range requests are not allowed to use `ETag` at all, only `Last-Modified`
Weak ETags are too short to encode a byte offset

questions.explanation: Range requests stitch a freshly fetched byte range onto bytes the client already holds. If the representation differs by even one byte, the result is corrupt — so the comparison must use a **strong** validator that promises exact equality. A weak ETag only promises *semantic* equivalence, which isn't enough to splice bytes safely; per the spec, weak validators must not be used where byte-range reassembly is involved. ETag length is irrelevant, and `ETag` is perfectly valid in `If-Range` — it just has to be strong.

questions.title: Why can `Last-Modified` (without an `ETag`) serve a stale body even though revalidation "succeeded"?

options.text: HTTP dates have one-second resolution, so two edits within the same second are indistinguishable
`Last-Modified` is always in the client's local time zone
Browsers ignore `Last-Modified` unless `ETag` is also present
`Last-Modified` only works for images, not HTML or JSON

questions.explanation: HTTP-date is formatted to whole seconds, so its resolution is exactly **1 second**. If a file is modified twice within the same second, the timestamp doesn't change, and `If-Modified-Since` returns `304` for content that actually changed — a stale serve. `ETag` sidesteps this because it can be derived from a content hash or version counter that changes on every edit. (HTTP dates are always GMT/UTC, not local; `Last-Modified` is content-type agnostic.) This sub-second blind spot is exactly why strong ETags are preferred for fast-changing resources.

questions.title: Which directive lets a cache return a stale response *immediately* while it revalidates in the background?
questions.description: It's a `Cache-Control` extension directive defined in RFC 5861. Write the token exactly as it appears in the header.
questions.explanation: `stale-while-revalidate=<seconds>` decouples freshness from latency: for up to N seconds *after* a response goes stale, the cache may serve the old copy instantly and kick off an async revalidation so the *next* request gets fresh data. Nobody waits on the origin. This HTTP directive is the namesake of the client-side "SWR" pattern (and the SWR React library). Its sibling, `stale-if-error`, covers a different failure mode — serving stale when the origin is *down* rather than merely stale.

questions.title: What does the user experience **700 seconds** after the response was stored?
questions.description: A response carries `Cache-Control: max-age=600, stale-while-revalidate=86400`.

options.text: The stale copy is returned immediately, and the cache revalidates in the background for next time
The request blocks until the origin returns a fresh copy
The cache returns a `504` because the entry is past `max-age`
The stale copy is discarded and a `200` with no body is returned

questions.explanation: At 700s the entry is stale (past `max-age=600`) but well inside the `stale-while-revalidate=86400` window. So the cache serves the stale body **with zero added latency** and asynchronously fetches a fresh copy to satisfy later requests. The user trades a little staleness for a fast response — ideal for dashboards and feeds where "instant but slightly old" beats "fresh but slow." Blocking on the origin is exactly the behaviour SWR is designed to avoid; a `504` would only appear under `stale-if-error` semantics when revalidation fails and no stale serve is permitted.

questions.title: Which directive lets a cache keep serving a stale copy when revalidation *fails* (origin error or unreachable)?

options.text: `stale-if-error`
`must-revalidate`
`no-cache`
`immutable`

questions.explanation: `stale-if-error=<seconds>` is a resilience directive: within its window, if revalidation hits a `5xx` or a connection failure, the cache may serve the last good (stale) response instead of surfacing the error — a graceful degradation that keeps the site up during an origin blip. It is almost the opposite of `must-revalidate`, which *forbids* serving stale once expired (and forces a `504` if it can't reach the origin). `no-cache` mandates revalidation before reuse; `immutable` suppresses revalidation while fresh.

questions.title: What is the practical effect of adding `must-revalidate` to `max-age=60`?

options.text: Once the entry is stale, the cache may not serve it without successful revalidation — even if the origin is unreachable
It forces revalidation on every request, even while the entry is still fresh
It doubles the freshness lifetime to 120 seconds
It allows shared caches to ignore `max-age`

questions.explanation: By default a cache is *allowed* (in some conditions) to serve stale content — e.g. when disconnected. `must-revalidate` removes that latitude: after `max-age` elapses, the stored copy may only be used if revalidation succeeds; if the origin can't be reached, the cache must respond `504 Gateway Timeout` rather than serve stale. It does **not** force revalidation while still fresh (that's what `no-cache` / `max-age=0` do) and it changes no lifetimes. Use it when serving stale would be genuinely harmful (e.g. pricing).

questions.title: What does `immutable` add over the year-long `max-age` alone?
questions.description: A fingerprinted asset is served with `Cache-Control: max-age=31536000, immutable`.

options.text: It suppresses the conditional revalidation the browser would otherwise send on an explicit reload while the entry is fresh
It makes the asset un-deletable from the cache until `max-age` expires
It encrypts the asset so intermediaries can't read it
It forces the browser to download a fresh copy on every reload

questions.explanation: When a user hits *Reload*, browsers normally fire a conditional request to check freshness — wasting a round trip for an asset that, by construction, can never change under its hashed URL (`app.7e3a91.js`). `immutable` tells the browser "don't bother revalidating while this is fresh," eliminating those reload-time `304`s. It doesn't pin the entry against eviction, encrypt anything, or force re-downloads — it removes work, it doesn't add it. This is the pattern that turns content-hashed bundles into truly free repeat loads.

questions.title: For how many hours will a cache treat this response as fresh?
questions.description: A response has **no** `Cache-Control` and **no** `Expires`, only a `Last-Modified`. The origin generated it (its `Date`) exactly **200 hours** after the resource was last modified. Use the common 10%-of-age heuristic that RFC 9111 suggests; enter a number.
questions.explanation: With no explicit freshness, a cache *may* compute a heuristic lifetime, and RFC 9111 suggests no more than a fraction of the interval since `Last-Modified`, with **10%** as the typical setting (the long-standing Apache `httpd` default). Here that interval is `Date − Last-Modified = 200 h`, so `0.10 × 200 = 20 hours`. The lesson: omitting caching headers does **not** mean "don't cache" — it hands the decision to opaque heuristics that can keep content far longer than you expect. Always set freshness explicitly.

questions.title: What does `Vary: Accept-Encoding` instruct caches to do?

options.text: Key stored variants by the request's `Accept-Encoding`, so a gzip client and an identity client get the right body
Refresh the response whenever the `Accept-Encoding` header changes server-side
Compress the response with whatever encoding the client prefers
Forbid caching unless the client sends `Accept-Encoding`

questions.explanation: `Vary` extends the cache key beyond the URL. `Vary: Accept-Encoding` means the same URL can map to multiple stored representations — one gzipped, one identity — and the cache must pick the variant matching the request's `Accept-Encoding`. Without it, a cache could hand a gzip body to a client that asked for none (or vice versa). `Vary` is a *keying* instruction, not a compression or refresh trigger — the origin still does the actual encoding; `Vary` just keeps the variants from colliding in the cache.

questions.title: What's the likely consequence of adding `Vary: User-Agent` to shared, cacheable HTML?

options.text: The hit rate collapses, because the near-infinite space of UA strings fragments the cache into countless variants
Caching speeds up, since each browser gets a dedicated fast path
Nothing changes; `User-Agent` is not a valid `Vary` value
The origin stops receiving any traffic at all

questions.explanation: Every distinct `User-Agent` string becomes a separate cache key. Because UA strings vary by browser, version, OS, and device in essentially unbounded combinations, the shared cache shatters into a huge number of one-hit variants — most requests miss and fall through to the origin, the opposite of what caching is for. The guidance is to `Vary` only on low-cardinality headers you truly branch on (e.g. `Accept-Encoding`). If you must adapt to device class, normalize first (e.g. an edge-computed `Vary: X-Device-Type` with a handful of values) rather than the raw `User-Agent`.

questions.title: What does `Age: 550` tell you?
questions.description: A response comes back from a CDN with `Cache-Control: max-age=600` and `Age: 550`.

options.text: The copy has been in caches for ~550 seconds, so it will be fresh for only ~50 more
The response will be deleted from the cache in 550 seconds
The origin server has been running for 550 seconds
The client must wait 550 seconds before using the response

questions.explanation: `Age` is a shared cache's estimate of how many seconds have elapsed since the origin generated (or last validated) the response. With `max-age=600` and `Age=550`, the remaining freshness is `600 − 550 = 50` seconds; after that the entry is stale and must be revalidated (or served per `stale-while-revalidate`, if present). A high `Age` relative to `max-age` is a useful signal that you're being served a well-aged edge copy — handy when debugging "why am I still seeing old content?"

questions.title: Which responses should a shared CDN cache refuse to store by default?
questions.description: Several responses arrive at a shared CDN cache. Select **every** one that applies.

options.text: `Cache-Control: private, max-age=600`
`Cache-Control: no-store`
A 200 response to a request that carried an `Authorization` header, with no `public`, `s-maxage`, or `must-revalidate`
`Cache-Control: public, max-age=600`

questions.explanation: Three of these are off-limits to a *shared* cache. `private` explicitly excludes shared caches. `no-store` forbids storage everywhere. And by default a shared cache must not store a response to an `Authorization`-bearing request unless a directive like `public`, `s-maxage`, or `must-revalidate` explicitly re-permits it — a guard against leaking authenticated content to other users. Only `public, max-age=600` is freely storable by the CDN. The `Authorization` rule is the subtle one and a frequent source of "why is my logged-in API response cached at the edge?" incidents.

questions.title: What is the "stampede" that follows a hot key's expiry?
questions.description: A very popular key expires in a Redis-backed cache. The pattern is also known as *thundering herd* or *dog-piling*.

options.text: Many concurrent requests all miss at once and hammer the origin to recompute the same value simultaneously
The cache evicts every other key to make room for the recomputed one
The CDN floods the cache with duplicate copies of the response
Clients retry so fast that the browser cache overflows to disk

questions.explanation: A stampede happens at the *moment of expiry* of a hot key: the cached value vanishes, and every in-flight request misses simultaneously, so they all stampede the origin (database, upstream API) to regenerate the **same** value at once. The redundant recomputation can saturate the backend and cause a latency spike or outage — precisely when traffic is highest. The fix space is about *collapsing* that concurrent work (next question), not about eviction or duplicate storage, which the distractors describe.

questions.title: Which techniques genuinely mitigate a cache stampede on a hot key?
questions.description: Select **every** technique that applies.

options.text: Request coalescing / single-flight: collapse concurrent identical misses into one origin fetch and share the result
Serve the stale value while a single worker revalidates in the background
Probabilistic early expiration: refresh a hot key slightly *before* it expires so one request, not all, recomputes
Add random jitter to TTLs so many keys don't expire at the same instant
Set `max-age=0` so every request always recomputes at the origin

questions.explanation: All four real mitigations share one idea — *don't let N requests do the same work N times*. **Single-flight** lets one request fetch while the rest await its result. **Serve-stale-while-revalidate** keeps answering from the old value so only one refresh runs. **Probabilistic early expiration** (the XFetch approach) has a single early request volunteer to recompute before expiry, so the key is never simultaneously cold and hot. **TTL jitter** desynchronizes mass-expiry so caches don't all miss together. Setting `max-age=0` does the opposite: it guarantees a permanent stampede by forcing every request to the origin.
questions.references: [Vattani, Chierichetti & Lowenstein: *Optimal Probabilistic Cache Stampede Prevention* (VLDB 2015)](https://www.vldb.org/pvldb/vol8/p886-vattani.pdf)

questions.title: What is this `404`-caching technique called, and what is its main risk?
questions.description: A lookup service caches `404 Not Found` results for a short time.

options.text: Negative caching: it shields the backend from repeated lookups of missing keys, but can briefly mask a resource that has since been created
Write-through caching: every miss is written back to the database
Cache poisoning: an attacker injects a fake 404 into the cache
Eviction: 404s are stored to decide which keys to drop first

questions.explanation: **Negative caching** stores the *non-existence* (or error) result — a `404`, an empty lookup, a DNS `NXDOMAIN` — so that a flood of requests for a missing key doesn't repeatedly pound the backend. (DNS formalizes this: negative answers are cached per the SOA `MINIMUM` TTL, RFC 2308.) The trade-off is freshness in the other direction: if the resource is created moments later, clients keep seeing the cached "not found" until the negative entry expires — so negative TTLs are kept short. It's unrelated to write-through (a write policy), poisoning (an attack), or eviction (a capacity policy).

questions.title: With request coalescing enabled, how many requests reach the origin?
questions.description: At an edge cache, 5,000 requests for the same uncached URL arrive within a few milliseconds.

options.text: One — the coalesced (single-flight) fetch; the other 4,999 wait and share its response
5,000 — coalescing only affects retries, not the first wave
Zero — coalescing serves all 5,000 from the empty cache
2,500 — coalescing halves origin load by batching pairs

questions.explanation: Request coalescing (also called *single-flight* or origin/request collapsing) detects that many concurrent requests want the *same* key, sends exactly **one** fetch to the origin, and fans the single response out to all waiters once it returns. So 5,000 near-simultaneous misses become **one** origin request. It's the most direct stampede defense for read-through caches and CDNs, and it's why a sudden traffic spike on a cold-but-cacheable URL doesn't translate into a proportional spike at the origin. (It can't serve from an empty cache, and it collapses the *first* wave, not just retries.)

questions.title: What's the difference between `staleTime` and `gcTime` in React Query?
questions.description: In TanStack Query (React Query) v5, `gcTime` is the option formerly called `cacheTime`.

options.text: `staleTime` is how long data is considered fresh (no background refetch); `gcTime` is how long an *inactive* query's data lingers before garbage collection
`staleTime` controls garbage collection; `gcTime` controls how long data is fresh
They are aliases — `gcTime` was simply renamed from `staleTime`
`staleTime` applies to mutations; `gcTime` applies to queries

questions.explanation: They govern two different clocks. **`staleTime`** is a *freshness* window: while data is fresh, React Query serves it from cache and skips background refetches; once stale, it refetches on triggers like remount or window refocus. **`gcTime`** is a *retention* window: once a query has no active observers (nothing on screen uses it), its cached data is kept for `gcTime` and then garbage-collected. A query can be stale yet still cached (eligible for instant display + revalidate), which is the whole stale-while-revalidate feel of the library. `gcTime` is the v5 rename of `cacheTime`; `staleTime` kept its name.

questions.title: Why does React Query refetch data it just rendered?
questions.description: A developer notices React Query shows cached data instantly on mount but immediately fires a network refetch. They set no options.

options.text: The default `staleTime` is `0`, so cached data is considered stale at once — it's shown immediately, then revalidated
The default `gcTime` is `0`, so the cache is wiped on every mount
React Query never caches `GET` requests without explicit configuration
It's a bug; data should never refetch when it's already in the cache

questions.explanation: React Query's default `staleTime` is **0 ms** — every query is stale the instant it resolves. So on remount it does the SWR two-step: serve the cached value immediately (no spinner), then refetch in the background to confirm freshness. That's intended behaviour, not a bug, and not a missing cache: `gcTime` defaults to **5 minutes**, so the data is very much cached. To stop the immediate refetch for data that changes slowly, raise `staleTime` (e.g. `staleTime: 60_000`). Conflating the two timers is the classic React Query gotcha.

questions.title: What happens when two components call `useSWR('/api/user')` within ~2 seconds?
questions.description: Vercel's `swr` React hook is named after the HTTP `stale-while-revalidate` pattern. Both components use the default configuration.

options.text: The requests are deduplicated into a single fetch (the `dedupingInterval`, default 2000 ms), and both components share the result
Each component fires its own independent request; SWR never dedupes
The second call throws, because a key may only be used by one component
Both calls block until a manual `mutate()` is invoked

questions.explanation: SWR keys requests by the cache key (`'/api/user'`) and, within its `dedupingInterval` (default **2000 ms**), collapses identical concurrent calls into **one** network request whose result is shared — the client-side echo of request coalescing. Combined with its core behaviour (return cached data first, then revalidate), this is exactly the HTTP `stale-while-revalidate` idea moved into the browser. Keys are explicitly meant to be *shared* across components, not exclusive, and nothing blocks waiting for `mutate()` (which is for manual cache updates/revalidation).

questions.title: Why does fingerprinting make cache *invalidation* almost a non-problem?
questions.description: Assets ship as content-hashed filenames (`app.7e3a91.js`) with `Cache-Control: max-age=31536000, immutable`.

options.text: A new build produces a new URL, so you never invalidate the old entry — you just stop referencing it; the URL *is* the version
`immutable` makes the CDN purge old files automatically on each deploy
The one-year `max-age` forces clients to revalidate every request anyway
Content hashes let the browser diff old and new bytes and patch in place

questions.explanation: Fingerprinting sidesteps the hard problem. Because the content hash is part of the filename, *any* change yields a *different URL*. The old URL's bytes never change, so caching it forever (`max-age=31536000, immutable`) is safe, and you "invalidate" simply by having the HTML reference the new hash — the stale entry is harmless and ages out on its own. There's no purge to coordinate, no revalidation round trip. This is why the classic advice is to cache the *HTML* shell briefly but cache its fingerprinted assets immutably: you turn invalidation into versioned naming, which a build tool gets right automatically.
